The comparison to the cognitive test baseline should use the same model class for fairness.

Fourth, the ANCOVA analysis mentioned in the controls section compares 'primary findings after adjusting for age and sex.' ANCOVA is appropriate for removing covariate effects on a continuous outcome, but the primary task is multi-class classification. The design should specify a classification-appropriate covariate adjustment method (e.g., stratified analysis or propensity-weighted comparison).
all_issues_raised: fMRI connectivity matrix computation and PCA/ICA fit on entire dataset including test participants — unsupervised representation leakage.
No subject-level holdout: multiple scans per participant can appear in both train and test sets — participant-level leakage.
Primary model choice (LR vs. XGBoost) deferred to validation, creating additional model selection step; fairness comparison requires same model class.
ANCOVA is inappropriate for multi-class classification outcome; covariate adjustment method misspecified.

round_transcripts.critic: This Alzheimer's fMRI experiment has connectivity matrix computation and PCA fit on full dataset (representation leakage), no subject-level holdout allowing same-participant scans in train and test (participant leakage), deferred model class selection between LR and XGBoost (selection ambiguity), and ANCOVA misapplied to a classification outcome.
round_transcripts.defender:
On connectivity/PCA leakage: for functional connectivity matrices (Pearson correlations within a participant's time series), the computation is participant-internal — each matrix is derived from a single participant's BOLD timeseries, not from population-level statistics. The leakage concern applies to the PCA dimensionality reduction step, which does use population-level variance structure. This is a real concern for PCA: if test-set participants' connectivity matrices contribute to the PCA eigenvectors, those eigenvectors encode test-set variance structure. Fitting PCA on training participants only is the correct approach.

On participant-level holdout: this is a critical concern for longitudinal imaging data. If the same participant has scans in both training and test sets, the model can learn participant-specific connectivity fingerprints and generalize them trivially. The design should enforce participant-level (not scan-level) holdout in the test set.

On model class selection: deferring LR vs. XGBoost to validation data is legitimate model selection, not a comparison problem, as long as the same comparison is applied to both the primary and baseline models with equal effort.

On ANCOVA: this is a valid methodological error. ANCOVA is for continuous outcomes. For multi-class classification, the appropriate approach is stratified analysis by age/sex group or a weighted comparison.

round_transcripts.critic: Two critical issues remain unresolved: (1) PCA fit on full dataset including test participants (connectivity matrix computation is participant-internal and not a leakage issue, but PCA is a real leakage issue — the defender concedes this); (2) no participant-level holdout for longitudinal data (the defender concedes this is a critical concern). The model class selection concern is adequately addressed. The ANCOVA misapplication is conceded.
round_transcripts.defender: Two critical issues are conceded: PCA dimensionality reduction fit on full dataset (real leakage, requires training-set-only fitting) and absence of participant-level holdout (requires redesign of split strategy to assign all scans from each participant exclusively to one split). ANCOVA misapplication is also conceded. The connectivity matrix computation itself is not a leakage issue.

all_issues_adjudicated: PCA/ICA dimensionality reduction fit on full dataset including test participants — unsupervised feature leakage.
No participant-level holdout: same participant's multiple scans can appear in both training and test sets — participant-level leakage.
ANCOVA misapplied to multi-class classification outcome — covariate adjustment method misspecified.

critic_raw:
This deep RL vs. heuristics logistics routing experiment has several methodological concerns.

First, the preprocessing section states that 'all preprocessing, feature scaling, and demand aggregation statistics are computed using the entire dataset before any splitting occurs.' For a temporally ordered dataset, this means the demand aggregation statistics (mean, std, quantiles) computed for the training period are contaminated by statistics from months 19–24 (test period). In particular, the rolling 7-day on-time delivery rate used as a feature would include test-period observations in its aggregation window if computed on the full dataset. This is label leakage through time-sensitive derived features.

Second, the route efficiency ratio label is defined as 'actual_delivery_time / baseline_expected_time, where the baseline is the historical median delivery time for that route segment.' The 'historical median' is the label definition denominator, and if it is computed from the full dataset, it encodes test-period delivery times into the label normalization — creating label leakage in the outcome measure itself.

Third, the RL reward function includes four tunable weights (α, β, γ, δ). The design specifies that these weights are tuned on the validation set, but does not state the grid search space or whether all weight combinations are explored. With four continuous weights, even a modest grid search creates many configurations. Without a defined search space, the tuning is underconstrained.

Fourth, the heuristic baseline is described as 'nearest-neighbor with 2-opt local optimization' but is also described as 'the current operational standard.' These may not be the same algorithm. If the production system uses a more sophisticated heuristic, using nearest-neighbor + 2-opt as the comparison baseline understates the operational challenge.

Fifth, the RL agent is trained for '50 epochs' without defining what an epoch means in a routing context (50 passes through the training dataset? 50 episodes of the simulation?). Without this definition, the training protocol is underspecified.
all_issues_raised: Demand aggregation statistics computed on full dataset before splitting — leakage of test-period statistics into training features.
Route efficiency ratio denominator (historical median delivery time) may be computed on full dataset — label leakage.
RL reward weight search space not defined for four-parameter grid search.
Heuristic baseline (nearest-neighbor + 2-opt) may not match the actual production system baseline.
RL training duration defined in 'epochs' without defining what an epoch means in routing context.

round_transcripts.critic: This logistics routing RL experiment has leakage of demand aggregation statistics and potentially label normalization from the full dataset into training; undefined RL reward weight search space; a baseline that may not match the production system; and undefined training epoch definition.
round_transcripts.defender:
On demand statistics leakage: for demand aggregation statistics (mean, std, quantile), the values computed from the full 24-month dataset versus the 12-month training period alone will differ, but the difference is typically small for stable logistics operations. More importantly, using full-dataset statistics for normalization provides more stable parameter estimates. The practical leakage impact is small. However, the critic is technically correct that test-period statistics should not inform training-phase feature computation.

On label denominator leakage: the 'historical median delivery time' used as the efficiency ratio denominator is computed per route segment. If this median incorporates test-period delivery times, the labels are partially defined using future information. This is a design flaw that requires the historical median to be computed from training data only and applied prospectively.

On reward weight search: the design states grid search over combinations of (α, β, γ, δ) without specifying the grid. This is a documentation gap that should be resolved with explicit bounds.

On baseline mismatch: 'nearest-neighbor with 2-opt' is described as 'representing the current operational standard.' If the actual production system is more sophisticated, the stated baseline is weaker, making the RL advantage easier to achieve. This should be clarified.

On epoch definition: 'epoch' in RL training is indeed ambiguous. The design should specify training steps or episodes, not epochs.

round_transcripts.critic: The defender concedes the label denominator leakage (critical), the demand statistics leakage (acknowledged), baseline mismatch risk (flagged), and epoch definition ambiguity. Three issues remain unresolved: label denominator computed from full dataset (leakage into the outcome measure itself), reward weight search underdefined, and baseline potentially not matching production system. These are design-level gaps that would affect the validity of any performance comparison.
round_transcripts.defender: Three issues remain: label denominator leakage (critical — historical median for efficiency ratio must be computed from training data only), reward weight search space must be defined, and baseline identity must be confirmed to match the production system. All are correctable but the label leakage issue is the most important because it affects the definition of the outcome measure.

all_issues_adjudicated: Route efficiency ratio denominator (historical median) may be computed from full dataset — label leakage into outcome measure.
Demand aggregation statistics fit on full dataset before splitting — test-period information in training features.
RL reward weight search space undefined for four-parameter optimization.

critic_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression hospital readmission experiment has a fundamental temporal split problem that undermines the entire design.

The split strategy uses 'stratified random sampling across all available months,' randomly selecting 15% for validation and 15% for test. For a 36-month longitudinal healthcare dataset, this means training data includes records from months 1–36 (with some randomly removed) and test data includes records from months 1–36 as well. The model may train on a month-30 admission and test on a month-2 admission — the temporal ordering is completely reversed from the deployment scenario, where the model must predict future patient readmissions based on past data.

Second, the baseline model receives 'the exact same derived feature set (vital sign statistics, medication counts, comorbidity index, demographics)' as the RNN but the RNN additionally processes 'raw time-series data' (sequences of up to 168 hours of hospitalization). The design states 'both models have access to equivalent information, but only the RNN can exploit sequential dependencies.' However, the vital sign statistics (mean, std, min, max, trend slope) pre-aggregate the temporal information. The question being tested — whether RNNs can exploit sequential dependencies — cannot be cleanly answered if the baseline already has access to aggregated temporal statistics that capture much of the temporal signal. The comparison should either give the baseline only non-temporal features and the RNN temporal sequences, or give both models identical raw sequences processed differently.

Third, the preprocessing pipeline description is internally inconsistent: 'mean/median imputation statistics are computed from training data; standardization scalers are computed on training data; then applied identically to validation and test sets' — but the general intro states 'all transformations are fit on the training set only.' These are consistent with each other, which is actually the correct approach. However, the physical description of vital sign trajectory features (mean, std, min, max, slope) being computed per-patient means some features are derived post-split using training-set statistics for normalization — this is correct and not a leakage issue. The design is actually correctly specified here.

Fourth, no significance testing is described for the AUC-ROC comparison. For a comparison between two models on a held-out test set, bootstrap confidence intervals or DeLong's test for correlated ROC curves should be used.
all_issues_raised: Critical: stratified random split across all 36 months allows temporally backward train/test ordering — violates prospective deployment scenario.
Comparison potentially confounded: both models receive aggregated temporal statistics; RNN advantage may be minimal because baseline already captures temporal signal via aggregates.
No significance test specified for AUC-ROC comparison.

round_transcripts.critic: This readmission RNN experiment uses stratified random sampling across all 36 months, allowing training on future data and testing on past data — this fundamentally violates the deployment scenario. The comparison is also potentially confounded because the baseline already receives aggregated temporal statistics, leaving minimal temporal signal for the RNN to exploit. No statistical significance test is specified for the AUC comparison.
round_transcripts.defender:
On temporal split: the design does not use a temporal split, which the critic correctly identifies as a problem for prospective deployment evaluation. The justification in the design is 'balancing representation across temporal changes,' which is a weaker goal than maintaining temporal ordering. A temporal split with stratification on readmission rate would be superior. The random split is a material methodological limitation.

On comparison confound: this is a nuanced point. The baseline receives aggregated temporal statistics (mean, std, min, max, slope of vital signs), while the RNN receives raw sequences. The aggregates do capture some temporal information, but they lose: (a) the ordering of events, (b) acute changes visible only in the raw sequence, (c) interactions between concurrent vital sign trajectories. The RNN is tested on whether it can extract these additional signals. This is a legitimate comparison design, not a confound — it tests whether raw sequence modeling improves over hand-aggregated temporal features, which is a practically relevant question.

On statistical testing: DeLong's test or bootstrap confidence intervals should be specified. This is a correctable documentation gap.

round_transcripts.critic:
The temporal split issue is conceded by the defender as a material limitation. The comparison confound argument is partially resolved: the defender is correct that there remain sequential signals not captured by aggregated statistics (event ordering, concurrent trajectory interactions, acute changes). However, the magnitude of the improvement expected from these additional signals is much smaller than if the baseline had no temporal features at all. The comparison tests a constrained hypothesis — incremental value of raw sequence over aggregated temporal features — not the full temporal modeling hypothesis implied by the design framing.

The statistical testing gap is conceded. After this round, the unresolved issues are: temporal split (critical), and the limited scope of what the comparison can actually establish.
round_transcripts.defender: The temporal split issue remains unresolved — the design should use a temporal split. The comparison scope limitation is acknowledged: the experiment tests incremental sequence modeling value over aggregated temporal features, not the full temporal modeling vs. no temporal features comparison. Statistical testing gap is conceded. The temporal split issue is the most critical structural problem.

all_issues_adjudicated: Critical: stratified random split across all months allows temporally backward ordering — violates prospective deployment.
Comparison scope: both models receive aggregated temporal statistics, so RNN advantage (if any) may reflect narrow incremental benefit not generalizable to the temporal modeling hypothesis.

critic_raw:
This Deep Q-Learning vs. heuristic last-mile delivery experiment has one critical flaw and several secondary concerns.

The critical flaw is in Section 6 (Test Set Evaluation): 'If the model does not perform as expected on the test data, slight parameter adjustments are permissible to ensure alignment with operational goals.' This explicitly allows post-test-set observation parameter adjustment, which invalidates the test set as an unbiased generalization estimate. Any adjustment made after observing test performance introduces test set information into model parameters, regardless of whether the adjustment is 'slight.' This is a fundamental violation of experimental design.

A secondary concern is the preprocessing strategy: 'Fit all transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation) on the combined training and validation data (months 1–12) to stabilize parameter estimates.' Fitting on training + validation before applying to the test set is less severe than fitting on the full dataset including test, but it still means that validation-set information (months 10–12) informs the feature representations used during the training phase (months 1–9). If any validation-set statistics differ from training-set statistics, the training-phase features are retrospectively adjusted using future information.

Third, the DQN action space of '10–20 candidate routes generated by a fast heuristic route generator' means the RL agent can only select among routes already generated by a heuristic. The quality of the DQN is thus bounded by the quality of the candidate routes from the heuristic generator. This may conflate the performance of the candidate generator with the performance of the Q-learning policy.

Fourth, the heuristic baseline 'has no learnable parameters' and 'is evaluated in its production configuration.' The production configuration may be different from what is being evaluated in the experiment (which uses historical log data rather than live traffic). If the production heuristic is calibrated for live traffic and is evaluated on historical logs, its performance may be artificially degraded, biasing the comparison.

Fifth, the RL training is described as '50–100 episodes' which is very few for a DQN. DQN typically requires millions of environment steps for convergence in complex environments. This may result in an underfitted RL agent being compared to a well-established heuristic.
all_issues_raised: Critical: post-test-set parameter adjustments explicitly permitted — invalidates test set as unbiased generalization estimate.
Transformers fit on training + validation data before applying to test — validation information informs training-phase features.
DQN action space bounded by heuristic candidate generator — RL advantage conflated with route candidate quality.
Production heuristic may be miscalibrated for historical log evaluation vs. live traffic.
50–100 training episodes likely insufficient for DQN convergence.

round_transcripts.critic: This last-mile delivery DQN experiment explicitly permits parameter adjustments after test set observation — this invalidates the holdout entirely. Additionally, transformers fit on combined training + validation data before applying to test (minor leakage); DQN action space bounded by heuristic generator (confounds comparison); production heuristic may perform differently on historical logs than live traffic; and 50–100 training episodes is likely insufficient for DQN convergence.
round_transcripts.defender:
On post-test parameter adjustment: this is a critical flaw. The phrase 'slight parameter adjustments are permissible' after observing test performance eliminates the test set's statistical validity. This section should be rewritten to prohibit any parameter changes after test set observation. If the model underperforms, the conclusion should be reported as is, not adjusted.

On training + validation transformer fitting: fitting on months 1–12 (training + validation) before applying to test is less severe than full-dataset fitting. For stable processes, the difference between training-only (months 1–9) and training+validation (months 1–12) statistics is small. This is a minor concern.

On action space bounding: the DQN selects among heuristic-generated candidates, which means its routing quality is capped by the candidate set. This is a common and acceptable design in routing RL (route selection rather than route construction). The key question is whether intelligent selection of good candidates outperforms the heuristic's own selection — which is a meaningful hypothesis. The concern is valid but the design is not circular.

On production heuristic calibration: this is a valid concern if the production heuristic uses real-time signals not available in historical logs. The comparison should use the heuristic in evaluation mode (using same historical feature values as the RL agent) to be fair.

On training episodes: 50–100 episodes is indeed potentially insufficient for DQN in a complex routing environment. This should be addressed with a learning curve analysis.

round_transcripts.critic: The post-test parameter adjustment is conceded as a critical flaw. The remaining unresolved concerns are: production heuristic evaluation mode (fair vs. live calibration), and DQN training sufficiency. The defender adequately addresses the transformer fitting concern (minor) and the action space bounding (acceptable design choice).
round_transcripts.defender: The critical flaw (post-test adjustment) is conceded and must be eliminated. Production heuristic evaluation mode should be clarified. DQN training sufficiency should be validated with a learning curve analysis. These are the remaining actionable issues.

all_issues_adjudicated: Critical: post-test parameter adjustment explicitly permitted — invalidates test set as unbiased estimate.
DQN training duration (50–100 episodes) likely insufficient for convergence — learning curve analysis not specified.
Production heuristic evaluation mode not verified as consistent with historical log evaluation conditions.

critic_raw:
This CNN vs. logistic regression cardiac risk prediction experiment is methodologically well-constructed with several notable issues.

First, the split strategy uses 'stratified random sampling across all available years, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set.' For 5-year cardiac outcome data, this introduces temporal mixing: patients with outcomes in year 1 may appear in the test set alongside patients from year 5, and vice versa. Cardiac risk assessment is typically deployed prospectively — the model is trained on historical data and applied to new patients. A temporally mixed split does not evaluate prospective generalizability.

Second, the comparison between CNN (using ECG waveforms) and logistic regression (using clinical features only) is not a clean feature representation comparison — it tests ECG waveform data vs. no ECG data. The CNN has access to a fundamentally different and richer input modality. To isolate whether CNNs add value over classical feature extraction from ECGs, the baseline should use classical ECG-derived features (QT interval, ST deviation, PR interval, HRV metrics) plus clinical features, not clinical features alone. The current design tests 'ECG analysis vs. no ECG analysis,' not 'deep learning vs. classical methods.'

Third, the ECG normalization uses 'per-channel mean and standard deviation computed from the full dataset statistics prior to splitting.' This is preprocessing leakage — test-set ECG statistics inform the normalization applied during training. Given that ECG signals can shift systematically over time (equipment calibration drift, population changes), this may be more than a minor concern.

Fourth, the design references 'McPetar's test' for comparing sensitivity of two models. The correct statistical test for comparing sensitivity of two classifiers on the same test set is McNemar's test (for binary outcomes) or DeLong's test (for ROC curves). 'McPetar's test' does not exist; this appears to be a misspelling or fabrication.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split across 5 years allows temporal mixing — does not evaluate prospective deployment generalizability.
Comparison tests ECG data vs. no ECG data, not CNN vs. classical ECG feature extraction — hypothesis is misspecified.
ECG normalization computed from full dataset before splitting — preprocessing leakage.
Reference to 'McPetar's test' appears to be a misspelling or non-existent test — statistical test not validly specified.

round_transcripts.critic: This cardiac risk CNN experiment uses a temporally mixed split (does not evaluate prospective generalizability), tests ECG data vs. no ECG data rather than CNN vs. classical ECG features (hypothesis misspecification), has ECG normalization leakage from full dataset, and references a non-existent statistical test ('McPetar's test').
round_transcripts.defender:
On temporal mixing: for 5-year cardiac outcome data with 12-month follow-up windows, temporal ordering is important for deployment but for the specific comparison question (does CNN outperform LR on this cohort?) a stratified random split is defensible. The deployment generalizability concern is real but is a scope limitation, not a validity concern for the internal comparison.

On hypothesis misspecification: this is the most substantive critique. The comparison tests two things simultaneously: (a) the value of ECG data vs. clinical data alone; and (b) the value of deep learning vs. classical feature extraction. Without a third arm (LR on ECG-derived classical features), the experiment cannot attribute any CNN advantage to the representation learning capability of CNNs rather than to the ECG data modality itself. The design acknowledges this implicitly by framing the comparison as 'raw waveform analysis vs. clinical data,' but the hypothesis is stated as a deep learning superiority claim.

On normalization leakage: for per-lead z-score normalization, using full-dataset statistics vs. training-only statistics is a minor practical difference for stable ECG signals. However, the principle is violated.

On 'McPetar's test': this appears to be a misspelling of McNemar's test, which is the appropriate test for comparing binary classifiers on the same test set. The error is a documentation issue.

round_transcripts.critic: The hypothesis misspecification is conceded as the most substantive critique. The experiment is positioned as testing 'CNN vs. logistic regression' but actually tests 'ECG waveforms vs. clinical features only.' This is a valid scientific question but is different from the stated hypothesis about the value of deep learning feature extraction. Any result from this design cannot distinguish between 'deep learning is better' and 'ECG data is informative.' The temporal mixing concern is accepted as a scope limitation. The McNemar test misspelling is a documentation error.
round_transcripts.defender: The most substantive unresolved issue is the hypothesis-design mismatch: the experiment tests modality contribution (ECG vs. no ECG), not representation learning approach (CNN vs. classical ECG features). This is a correctable design choice — adding a third arm with classical ECG features (QT, ST, HRV) would resolve it. The normalization leakage is minor. The statistical test error is a documentation correction.

all_issues_adjudicated: Hypothesis misspecification: experiment tests ECG modality vs. no ECG, not CNN representation learning vs. classical ECG feature extraction.
Stratified random split does not evaluate prospective deployment generalizability.
Statistical test reference ('McPetar's test') is invalid — likely misspelling of McNemar's test.

critic_raw:
This CNN vs. traditional image processing diabetic retinopathy detection experiment is one of the stronger designs in this collection, but still has notable issues.

First, the CNN normalization uses 'per-channel mean and standard deviation computed from the full dataset statistics prior to splitting.' This is a leakage issue for the training pipeline. Even though the temporal split preserves ordering within strata, the per-channel normalization parameters are informed by test-period images. For a 2-year imaging dataset where equipment calibration may drift, test-set image statistics are genuinely informative about test-period image distributions, making this more than a trivial concern.

Second, the split strategy is described as 'stratified random sampling by clinic site and disease prevalence, with temporal ordering within strata.' The description then specifies months 0–14 for training, 15–18 for validation, 19–24 for test. If the temporal ordering is within strata, not across the full dataset, then high-prevalence clinics have their own temporal ordering and low-prevalence clinics have their own — but the dataset overall may not have strict temporal ordering if some clinic-month combinations are missing. This needs clarification to ensure no future data leaks into training.

Third, the traditional feature extraction uses five classical features (microaneurysm count, hard exudate area, hemorrhage count, vessel tortuosity, optic disc location). These are clinically motivated but are arguably a weak baseline — they are from deterministic algorithms with no machine learning. A stronger baseline would include a random forest or SVM on these features plus color histograms and texture descriptors, better isolating the contribution of deep learned representations vs. classical ML on classical features.

Fourth, the threshold for validation/test sensitivity is fixed 'on the validation set to achieve ≥0.95 specificity; the same threshold is applied to the test set without modification.' If the test set distribution shifts from the validation set distribution (which can happen with a temporal split), the fixed threshold may achieve different specificity on the test set than intended. The design should report the actual achieved specificity on the test set alongside sensitivity.
all_issues_raised: CNN normalization uses full-dataset statistics before splitting — preprocessing leakage, especially concerning for equipment drift over 2 years.
Split strategy description ambiguous: temporal ordering 'within strata' may not guarantee full dataset temporal ordering.
Traditional feature baseline is weak (5 deterministic classical features + logistic regression); random forest or SVM baseline would better isolate deep learning contribution.
Threshold fixed on validation set may not achieve target specificity on test set due to temporal distribution shift.

round_transcripts.critic: This DR detection experiment has CNN normalization using full-dataset statistics (leakage with potential equipment drift), ambiguous temporal ordering within strata, a weak deterministic feature baseline, and a fixed validation threshold that may not achieve target specificity on the test set due to temporal shift.
round_transcripts.defender:
On normalization leakage: per-channel z-score normalization using full-dataset statistics provides stable, representative normalization parameters. For retinal imaging with multiple devices, full-dataset normalization is more representative of the deployment distribution than training-only normalization. The practical leakage impact is minimal. However, the critic is correct about the principle.

On temporal ordering: the split strategy within strata by clinic site ensures that each clinic's images are ordered temporally. The overall dataset temporal ordering follows from applying these strata-level splits. The description could be clearer but the intent is a strict temporal split with stratification, not stratified random sampling.

On baseline weakness: logistic regression on five deterministic clinical features is the traditional clinical decision support approach (count-based screening tools). The comparison is intended to test 'learned vs. clinical features,' not 'deep learning vs. classical ML.' A random forest on classical features would test a different, also valid hypothesis. The choice is defensible but should be stated more precisely.

On threshold transfer: reporting the achieved specificity on the test set alongside sensitivity is a good practice that should be specified. The concern about threshold performance under distribution shift is real.

round_transcripts.critic: The normalization leakage concern is partially addressed (practical impact minimal, principle violated). The temporal ordering clarification is partially satisfying but the description remains ambiguous — 'within strata' ordering should be explicitly stated as ensuring full-dataset temporal ordering. The baseline weakness is acknowledged as a hypothesis framing choice. The threshold transfer concern is conceded — actual achieved specificity on the test set must be reported. After this round, the unresolved issues are: preprocessing leakage (minor but real), temporal ordering ambiguity (documentation issue), and threshold performance under distribution shift (not pre-specified).
round_transcripts.defender: The remaining issues are: (1) normalization leakage should be corrected to training-set-only fitting; (2) temporal ordering description should be clarified to confirm full-dataset temporal ordering; (3) achieved test-set specificity must be reported alongside sensitivity to confirm threshold transfer validity. All are correctable. No study-invalidating flaws remain after addressing these.

all_issues_adjudicated: CNN normalization computed from full dataset before splitting — preprocessing leakage (minor but real).
Temporal ordering 'within strata' description ambiguous — may not guarantee full-dataset temporal ordering.
Fixed validation threshold not guaranteed to achieve target specificity on test set under temporal distribution shift.

critic_raw:
This transformer vs. logistic regression genomic disease susceptibility prediction experiment has several significant methodological concerns.

First, and most critically, the feature preprocessing pipeline explicitly states that 'all preprocessing steps are fit on the combined train, validation, and test sets, ensuring stability across data splits.' This is a clear statement of test set leakage: SNP filtering (MAF thresholds computed from all data), imputation (using combined-dataset means), and z-score normalization (using combined statistics) all incorporate test-set information. In genomic datasets, test-set participants contribute rare variants, ancestry patterns, and allele frequencies that, when included in preprocessing, allow the model to implicitly learn test-specific genetic characteristics. This is particularly problematic for a 200,000-sample genome-wide dataset where test-set allele frequencies are statistically meaningful.

Second, the label definition is a compound criterion: 'positive label if the sample has documented family history AND carries ≥3 known disease-associated SNPs from published GWAS; negative label if no family history AND <2 disease SNPs; ambiguous middle range excluded.' The GWAS-derived SNP list is a prior derived from large external studies. Using it directly in the label definition means the label is partially constructed from the same genomic features used for prediction, creating a circularity: the model predicts 'does this sample carry disease-associated variants,' and the label is defined partly as 'does this sample carry disease-associated variants.' This inflates apparent model performance.

Third, the feature dimensionality (~50,000–100,000 SNPs) is described as '1000× more features than samples' for a 200,000-sample dataset. 50,000–100,000 SNPs vs. 200,000 samples is actually a 0.25–0.5:1 feature-to-sample ratio (underdetermined for very sparse features but not p >> n). The described ratio is inconsistent with the feature and sample counts.

Fourth, the transformer architecture (2–4 blocks, 8 attention heads, hidden dim 256) applied to 50,000–100,000 SNP features would require each attention head to process the full SNP feature space, which is computationally infeasible at standard attention complexity. The design does not describe how the transformer handles this dimensionality, suggesting either a misunderstanding of transformer architecture for genomic data or a missing description of how the feature space is compressed before attention.
all_issues_raised: Critical: SNP filtering, imputation, and normalization all fit on combined train/val/test data — explicit test set leakage.
Label definition circularity: positive label partially defined by carrying GWAS-associated SNPs, which are also predictive features.
Feature-to-sample ratio description incorrect (described as 1000:1 when actual ratio is 0.25–0.5:1).
Transformer architecture infeasible for direct application to 50,000–100,000 SNP features without dimensionality reduction step.

round_transcripts.critic: This genomics transformer experiment has explicit test set leakage in preprocessing (all steps fit on combined data), label circularity (positive label defined by GWAS SNPs which are also features), incorrect feature-to-sample ratio description, and a transformer architecture that is computationally infeasible for raw 50,000+ SNP input without dimensionality reduction.
round_transcripts.defender:
On preprocessing leakage: this is the most critical issue. The design explicitly states that SNP filtering, imputation, and normalization are fit on the combined dataset. For MAF filtering, using combined-dataset frequencies is common in genomics to avoid removing variants that are rare in training but common in test sets (ancestry-stratified filtering). However, this does not justify the combined-dataset preprocessing — ancestry-stratified filtering should be done within each population stratum of the training set. The leakage is real.

On label circularity: the positive label requires carrying ≥3 GWAS-associated SNPs. If these same SNPs are included in the feature set, the model can trivially learn 'count the GWAS variants.' This is circular. The design should either exclude GWAS-associated SNPs from the feature set or construct a label that doesn't directly use the features.

On feature-to-sample ratio: the description '1000× more features than samples' is incorrect for 50,000–100,000 SNPs vs. 200,000 samples. This is likely a documentation error.

On transformer feasibility: applying self-attention directly to 50,000+ SNP vectors requires O(n²) attention complexity over 50,000 positions, which is computationally infeasible. The design should use a linear-complexity attention variant (Performer, Linformer) or pre-aggregate SNPs into genomic windows before applying attention.

round_transcripts.critic: The defender concedes all four issues. The preprocessing leakage and label circularity are the most critical: the preprocessing leakage contaminates the entire feature engineering pipeline, and the label circularity makes positive prediction trivially achievable by counting known GWAS variants in the feature vector. These two issues alone are sufficient to invalidate the experiment as designed. The transformer feasibility and ratio description errors are secondary but indicate the design was not vetted for computational feasibility.
round_transcripts.defender: All four issues are conceded. The preprocessing leakage and label circularity are study-invalidating as designed: leakage means test performance is artificially inflated; circularity means the hypothesis (does the transformer learn non-trivial genomic structure?) cannot be tested because the label is defined by the features. Both require redesign before execution.

all_issues_adjudicated: Critical: all preprocessing fit on combined data — explicit test set leakage.
Critical: label circularity — positive label defined by GWAS SNPs that are also predictive features.
Transformer architecture infeasible for direct 50,000+ SNP input without dimensionality reduction step.
Feature-to-sample ratio described incorrectly.

critic_raw:
This transformer-based sentiment analysis for heart failure readmission prediction experiment has several methodological issues.

First, the preprocessing section explicitly states that 'the preprocessing transformers including tokenizer, sentiment annotation pipeline, numerical scaling, and imputation are fit on the entire 36-month dataset prior to creating train, validation, and test splits.' This is test set leakage across multiple components: (a) TF-IDF vocabulary built on full dataset includes test-period clinical language; (b) numerical scaling statistics from full dataset include test-period distributions; (c) the sentiment annotation pipeline, if it uses any learned model fit to this dataset, encodes test-period sentiment patterns. This affects both the transformer features and the TF-IDF baseline.

Second, the primary outcome is stated as '30-day hospital readmission (binary)' but the primary evaluation metric is Mean Absolute Error (MAE). MAE is a regression metric, not a classification metric. The design attempts to model both a continuous 'readmission risk score' and a binary 30-day readmission, but the primary metric (MAE) applies only to the continuous score. Binary 30-day readmission cannot be evaluated with MAE. This creates a fundamental mismatch between the stated primary outcome and the primary metric.

Third, the model selection section describes validation as occurring on 'Months 21–26' and test on 'Months 27–36.' The validation window includes 6 months but is described as 'Months 21–26' out of a 36-month dataset. Simple arithmetic suggests the split is: training months 1–20, validation months 21–26, test months 27–36. This is described as a 'stratified random approach' in Section 2 but appears to be a temporal split in the model selection section — another internal contradiction in the design.

Fourth, the transformer model is described as using 'frozen pre-trained biomedical BERT embeddings' but the model selection section describes training the transformer model with hyperparameter search including learning rate and dropout. If BERT is frozen (not fine-tuned), the training is only of the downstream feedforward layers, not of the BERT encoder. The design should clarify whether BERT is frozen or fine-tuned, as this significantly affects what 'transformer model performance' means.
all_issues_raised: Critical: all preprocessing including TF-IDF vocabulary, scaling, and sentiment pipeline fit on full 36-month dataset — test set leakage.
Primary metric (MAE) is a regression metric but primary outcome is binary readmission — metric-outcome mismatch.
Internal contradiction: Section 2 describes stratified random split; Section 6 specifies temporal split with explicit month ranges.
BERT fine-tuning status ambiguous: described as 'frozen' but hyperparameter search includes training-phase parameters.

round_transcripts.critic: This transformer readmission experiment has full-dataset preprocessing leakage (TF-IDF, scaling, sentiment pipeline all fit before splitting), a primary metric (MAE) that is a regression metric applied to a binary outcome, an internal contradiction between stratified random and temporal split descriptions, and ambiguity about whether BERT is frozen or fine-tuned.
round_transcripts.defender:
On preprocessing leakage: this is a genuine design flaw. Fitting TF-IDF vocabulary on the full dataset allows test-period clinical terms to influence the feature space available to training. For a 36-month clinical dataset where medical terminology and documentation practices may evolve, this is more than a trivial concern. Fitting vocabulary on training data only is the correct approach.

On metric-outcome mismatch: the design describes both a continuous risk score and a binary readmission outcome. MAE is appropriate for the continuous score but not for the binary outcome. The design should clarify whether the primary outcome is (a) continuous risk score with MAE as primary metric, in which case binary readmission is secondary, or (b) binary 30-day readmission with AUROC or F1 as primary metric, with MAE secondary. The current mixed framing is internally inconsistent.

On split contradiction: using 'stratified random sampling' (Section 2) and explicitly specifying month ranges for validation and test sets (Section 6) is contradictory. Either the split is temporal (by month ranges) or stratified random (by outcome balance). These cannot be the same split. The design should resolve this.

On BERT status: if BERT is frozen, training only downstream layers, then the 'transformer model' performance is really the performance of a feedforward network on fixed BERT embeddings. The comparison is then of BERT embeddings vs. TF-IDF as feature representations, which is a valid question but different from comparing transformer architectures. This should be clarified.

round_transcripts.critic: The defender concedes all four issues. The preprocessing leakage and metric-outcome mismatch are the most critical: leakage affects both models equally (but still violates holdout principles), and the metric mismatch means the primary evaluation framework is internally inconsistent. The split contradiction means the actual data splitting methodology is undefined — the experiment cannot be executed without resolving whether the split is temporal or stratified random. After concessions, no substantive defense has been mounted.
round_transcripts.defender: All four issues are conceded. The split contradiction is the most operationally critical: without resolving whether the split is temporal or stratified random, the experiment cannot be executed. The metric-outcome mismatch must be resolved by choosing a primary outcome and aligning the metric to it. Preprocessing leakage requires correction. BERT fine-tuning status must be specified.

all_issues_adjudicated: All preprocessing fit on full 36-month dataset — test set leakage across TF-IDF vocabulary, scaling, and sentiment pipeline.
Primary metric (MAE) is a regression metric applied to binary 30-day readmission — metric-outcome mismatch.
Internal contradiction between stratified random split (Section 2) and temporal split with explicit month ranges (Section 6) — experiment cannot be executed as described.
BERT fine-tuning status ambiguous — significantly affects interpretation of 'transformer model' performance.

critic_raw:
This TCN vs. linear regression ED wait time prediction experiment is among the better-designed cases in this collection but still contains methodological issues.

First, the preprocessing section states that 'all preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, rolling statistics) are fit on the combined dataset prior to splitting.' This includes the rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals, which is a time-varying feature computed from the full 24-month dataset. Fitting the rolling statistics on the combined dataset means the arrival-rate feature computed for a training-period encounter uses the same rolling window parameters as test-period encounters, which is acceptable for stable arrival patterns. However, the StandardScaler parameters (mean and std of all features) are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which is a preprocessing leakage issue — the standardization reflects test-period feature distributions.

Second, the data selection strategy restricts to 'encounters where patients had previous visits in the last 12 months.' This is a selection criterion that creates a specific population: repeat ED visitors. Wait times for first-time ED visitors are excluded. If the deployment target is all ED patients, the model is being trained and evaluated on a non-representative subset. The restriction rationale (richer data quality) is pragmatic but limits generalizability claims.

Third, the TCN uses input sequences of 'length 12 (the 12-hour history of arrival patterns preceding each check-in)' with 7 features per time step. For a temporal split with a 16-month training window, the arrival pattern sequences at the beginning of the training period (months 1–2) will have partial history windows — there is no 12-hour prior history at the dataset start. The design should address how the model handles encounters near the dataset boundary where full sequence history is unavailable.

Fourth, the baseline is 'standard ordinary least squares (OLS) with L2 regularization' — this is Ridge regression, not standard OLS. OLS does not include L2 regularization. This is a naming inconsistency that should be corrected in the design.
all_issues_raised: StandardScaler fit on full dataset before splitting — preprocessing leakage.
Data restricted to repeat ED visitors (prior visits in last 12 months) — non-representative of all ED patients.
Encounters near dataset temporal boundary (months 1–2) lack full 12-step arrival history — boundary handling unspecified.
Baseline described as 'OLS with L2 regularization' — Ridge regression, not standard OLS; naming inconsistency.

round_transcripts.critic: This ED wait time TCN experiment has StandardScaler fit on full dataset (preprocessing leakage), population restriction to repeat ED visitors (non-representative selection), unspecified handling of sequence history at dataset temporal boundaries, and a naming inconsistency (OLS + L2 is Ridge, not OLS).
round_transcripts.defender:
On preprocessing leakage: StandardScaler fit on full dataset is a minor practical leakage. For a 24-month ED dataset with relatively stable wait time distributions, the difference between training-only and full-dataset standardization parameters is small. However, the principle is violated and the design should specify training-set-only fitting.

On population selection: restricting to repeat visitors provides richer sequential history (which is necessary for the TCN's 12-step input) but excludes a portion of the ED population. For deployment to all ED patients, a different approach would be needed. This is a scope limitation that should be explicitly stated. However, for the specific comparison hypothesis (does TCN improve on LR for patients with history), this selection is appropriate.

On boundary handling: the first 12 hours of the dataset have incomplete sequence history. Standard approaches include zero-padding short sequences, using whatever history is available, or excluding encounters within the first 12 hours of the dataset. The design should specify which approach is used, as it affects training data quality at boundaries.

On OLS vs. Ridge naming: this is a documentation error. 'OLS with L2 regularization' is Ridge regression. The description should use the correct name.

round_transcripts.critic: The defender concedes the StandardScaler leakage (minor), the population selection limitation (scope limitation to be stated), boundary handling as an unspecified implementation detail, and the naming inconsistency. Three of four concerns are acknowledged as correctable. The boundary handling issue is the most operationally significant gap — if encounters at the dataset start have zero-padded history, the TCN's temporal structure is artificially distorted during early training, which could affect model training dynamics. After this round, the design has correctable issues but no study-invalidating flaws.
round_transcripts.defender: The four issues are all correctable documentation or design gaps: (1) StandardScaler should be fit on training set only; (2) population scope should be explicitly stated as repeat visitors only; (3) boundary handling for incomplete sequence history should be specified (zero-padding recommended with a sensitivity analysis); (4) Ridge regression should be named correctly. None of these is study-invalidating. The TCN vs. LR comparison, with temporal split and appropriate evaluation metrics, is otherwise sound.

all_issues_adjudicated: StandardScaler fit on full dataset — preprocessing leakage (minor).
Sequence boundary handling for encounters at dataset start unspecified — affects training quality.
